channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. Tells Citizens to Leave Iraq After Second Attack on Embassy
item.description: An Iran-backed militia took responsibility for an overnight attack on the U.S. Embassy in Baghdad. President Trump called on other nations to help escort oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Ohad Zwigenberg/Associated Press

item.title: Netanyahu Has the War He Always Wanted, but on Trump’s Terms
item.description: Israel’s prime minister wanted regime change in Iran. But President Trump seems prepared to settle for something less.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in January at the funeral of an Israeli hostage.

item.title: Mahmood Mamdani, Father of NYC Mayor, Is Writing a Book on Gaza
item.description: At once, Mahmood Mamdani’s fame was eclipsed by his son’s. At the same time, the election of Zohran Mamdani has attracted new interest in his father’s work.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:credit: Brian Otieno for The New York Times
item.media:description: Since his days as a scholar in Tanzania in the 1970s, Mahmood Mamdani has wrestled with subjects including the nature of colonialism and its enduring consequences.

item.title: His Film Is Spain’s Submission to the Oscars. He’s Not Sure How Spanish It Is.
item.description: Spanish cinema has entered a new and more diverse era, film experts say. Oliver Laxe, the director of Oscar-nominated “Sirat,” embodies the shift.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Spanish director Oliver Laxe questioned “if my cinema is Spanish or not,” and said that his Oscar-nominated film, “Sirat,” differed from the work of his Spanish peers.

item.title: Explosion at Jewish School in Amsterdam Is 2nd Antisemitic Attack in 2 Days, Officials Say
item.description: No one was hurt in the explosion at a Jewish school in Amsterdam, or in an earlier one at a Rotterdam synagogue.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Piroschka Van De Wouw/Reuters
item.media:description: Police officers on Saturday outside the Amsterdam school where a blast damaged an outer wall. The mayor said Jews in Amsterdam were “increasingly confronted with antisemitism.”

item.title: Is Latin America Ready to Abandon Cuba?
item.description: Latin America’s left saw Cuba as its lodestar. Now leaders across the spectrum are hesitant to aid a nation in the Trump administration’s cross hairs.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: The Capitolio Nacional in Havana. Cuba’s isolation is deepening in the region.

item.title: To Fight Iran’s Drones, U.S. Taps Ukraine’s Hard-Earned Knowledge
item.description: As Iran has shown, warfare involves far more deadly projectiles than it once did. It’s a problem Ukraine has been dealing with for years.
item.dc:creator: Michael Schwirtz
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kacper Pempel/Reuters
item.media:description: An American soldier, in Poland, carrying an interceptor drone used in the Merops anti-drone system. The system was deployed in Poland after Russian drones crossed into the country.

item.title: Cuba’s History Since the Revolution, in Photos
item.description: The Cuban government, which has so far outlasted 13 U.S. presidents, faces its gravest challenge yet. Images from The New York Times and others record nearly seven decades of political turmoil, economic crises and small moments of ordinary life.
item.dc:creator: Craig Allen and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: Old Havana, the capital’s historic center, is a warren of faded facades and sunlit plazas.

item.title: Surge in Oil Prices Shakes Pakistan’s Already Fragile Economy
item.description: Families preparing for Eid al-Fitr and farmers ready for harvest are being squeezed in a country that gets nearly all its oil through the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arif Ali/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A fuel storage site in Lahore, Pakistan, on Tuesday.

item.title: How the War in Iran Could Help China and Change Asia
item.description: American officials have said for years that they would prioritize the Indo-Pacific. Now they’re moving warships, missiles and air defenses out for a war in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave, Choe Sang-Hun, Javier C. Hernández and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Fazry Ismail
item.media:description: Fighter jets on the aircraft carrier U.S.S. Abraham Lincoln in 2024. A carrier group including the ship was redirected from the South China Sea to the Middle East in January.

item.title: Cuba Acknowledges Talks with Trump Officials For the First Time
item.description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel, whose country is rapidly running out of fuel, said the talks were based on “respect for the political systems of both countries.”
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles, Patricia Mazzei and Dariel Pradas
item.media:credit: Adalberto Roque/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba speaking in Havana in January.

item.title: A Visit to a Temple at the Heart of the Thailand-Cambodia Conflict
item.description: A rare visit to a Khmer temple on Thailand and Cambodia’s border showed how deadly clashes between the two countries have scarred a heritage site.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee and Lauren DeCicca
item.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times

item.title: Phones ‘Ringing Off the Hook’ for Ukraine Defense Firms as Mideast Seeks Help
item.description: Ukraine wants to leverage its defense expertise into security partnerships and to reap potentially vast profits for its arms industry.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer, Maria Varenikova and Brendan Hoffman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Demonstrating the Bullet interceptor drone in the Kyiv region of Ukraine on Wednesday.

item.title: Two Arrested in Death of Anti-Iranian Regime Activist in Canada
item.description: A man and a woman were charged with murder in what the authorities described as a “targeted incident” against Masood Masjoody, who had gone missing weeks ago in British Columbia.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.category: Iran Front for the Revival of Law and National Sovereignty
item.media:credit: Sebastien St-Jean/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Trump adds to speculation about Iranian leader’s condition.
item.dc:creator: Traci Carl and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: During a rally in Tehran on Friday, a woman held a photo of Mojtaba Khamenei, Iran’s new supreme leader, in front of a banner showing his father, who was killed in recent U.S.-Israeli strikes.

item.title: Marseille’s Tight Mayoral Race Is a Bellwether for France’s Future
item.description: Voters across France choose mayors this week. The far right is performing strongly in the country’s second city, making the contest there a test of national shifts.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ana Castelain
item.media:credit: James Hill for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Benoît Payan, the incumbent mayor of Marseille, France, distributing leaflets in the city on Friday. Mr. Payan is competing in a tight race featuring a strong far-right candidate.

item.title: In Scolding the New U.S. Ambassador, South Africa Signals Defiance
item.description: The latest clash between the two countries illustrates the depths to which relations have sunk and the struggle to forge a new path forward.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon
item.media:credit: William Widmer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brent Bozell in New Orleans in 2019.

item.title: Iceland’s Chief ‘Lava Cooler’ Is Bracing for the Next Volcanic Eruption
item.description: Helgi Hjorleifsson, a firefighter, is a leader in a national experiment to steer rivers of lava away from important sites. Some called it crazy, but it worked.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Egill Bjarnason
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times
item.media:description: Helgi Hjorleifsson driving through lava fields in southwestern Iceland in February.

item.title: Some Olympic Leaders Want to See Fixed Winter Games Host Cities
item.description: Fewer cities are bidding for Olympics, and those that are can’t always accommodate every event. The Milan-Cortina Games were the most sprawling Olympics in history.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: The International Olympic Committee has had to pivot toward flexibility as the number of bidders for its marquee winter event has started to dwindle.

item.title: Three More Iranian Soccer Players Withdraw Bid for Asylum in Australia
item.description: Seven members of the national women’s team had sought refuge in the country after they were labeled ‘traitors’ at home. Four of them have since changed their minds.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Izhar Khan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Iranian women’s national soccer team before a match in Australia this month.

item.title: Warning to Americans to leave Iraq is a sign the Iran war is spilling over the border.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar and Eve Sampson
item.media:credit: Hadi Mizban/Associated Press
item.media:description: U.S. embassy personnel inspect the damage caused by an attack on the building in Baghdad on Saturday.

item.title: F.C.C. Chair Threatens to Revoke Broadcasters’ Licenses Over War Coverage
item.description: The comment from Brendan Carr came on the heels of a social media message from President Trump criticizing the news media’s coverage of the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brendan Carr in Washington in January.

item.title: Here’s What Happened in the U.S.-Israel War With Iran on Saturday
item.description: The U.S. Embassy in Baghdad was attacked overnight. Israeli strikes continued to hit the capitals of Iran and Lebanon, as Iran refused to relinquish control over a vital oil route.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officials said that more than 800 people have been killed in Lebanon since the start of the war, with more than 2,100 people killed across the region.

item.title: Tehran Seems Largely Empty, as Many Flee and Others Weary of the Fighting
item.description: Residents say a heavy, lifeless atmosphere hangs over the streets of Iran’s capital, which has endured relentless attacks throughout the war.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Tajrish Bazaar in Tehran on Saturday. More than 1,348 civilians have been killed in Iran since the beginning of the war, according to the country’s representative to the United Nations.

item.title: Sandstorm Batters Gaza, Slamming Makeshift Shelters
item.description: Millions of Gazans have remained without proper housing since the war, according to the United Nations.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:credit: Dawoud Abu Alkas/Reuters
item.media:description: Displaced Palestinians faced a sandstorm in Gaza City on Saturday.

item.title: The war has put Middle East water supplies at risk.
item.dc:creator: Mira Rojanasakul, Claire Brown and Hiroko Tabuchi

item.title: Dozens of Medical Workers Killed as Israel Hits Lebanon
item.description: Lebanese health officials say that 31 health workers have been killed by Israeli strikes. Israel claimed, without evidence, that Hezbollah has been using ambulances and medical facilities.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Mohammed Zaatari/Associated Press
item.media:description: The funeral for Youssef Assaf, a volunteer with the Lebanese Red Cross, in Tyre, Lebanon, on Wednesday.

item.title: Lebanon’s Displaced Flee Israeli Strikes One Year Into War
item.description: Israeli calls for the evacuation of huge swaths of southern Lebanon have led 830,000 people, about 14 percent of Lebanon’s population, to flee.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A man lighting a fire to keep warm along Beirut’s waterfront, after fleeing the city’s southern suburbs last week.

item.title: This is what happened on March 13.
What’s Good?
item.description: Introducing The Good List, a new weekly newsletter by Melissa Kirsch designed to bring joy and meaning to your days.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Canada Takes Its Sovereignty Push to Space
item.description: The country aims to expand domestic satellite systems and cut dependence on American networks like Elon Musk’s Starlink.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Pat Kane for The New York Times
item.media:description: The northern lights over Yellowknife, Northwest Territories. Scientists worry that space is becoming cluttered with satellites and debris.

item.title: How My Trip to Antarctica Unlocked a Family Discovery
item.description: An eight-week voyage to the bottom of the Earth helped the photojournalist Chang W. Lee better understand his late father.
item.dc:creator: Chang W. Lee

item.title: How Migrant Workers Have Been Affected by Iran’s Strikes
item.description: Since the U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran began, Iran has fired hundreds of missiles and drones in retaliation at Gulf countries. Iranian officials say that they are attacking U.S. military bases and American interests in the Gulf countries, not civilian targets. Our reporter Vivian Nereim talks with Katrin Bennhold about how migrant workers have been affected.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Katrin Bennhold, Leila Medina, James Surdam and Parin Behrooz
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump and Rubio’s Vision of War: The Art of Destroy and Deal
item.description: Unleashing his inner hawk, Secretary of State Marco Rubio plans President Trump’s military interventions. So far, they favor regime compliance, not change.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio is at the helm of President Trump’s aggressive campaigns to reshape the governments of Iran, Venezuela, Cuba and beyond.

item.title: What to Know About Kharg Island, Iran’s Key Oil Hub
item.description: The Persian Gulf island, targeted by U.S. strikes, is home to Iran’s main oil export terminal. Any disruption could jolt global energy markets.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Megan Specia
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu Agency, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Smoke billowing from a flare stack at the Port of Kharg Island oil terminal in Iran in 2017.

item.title: Here is the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Yeganeh Torbati, Farnaz Fassihi and Erika Solomon

item.title: Trump’s War Alliance With Israel Is Reshaping the Middle East. But It Carries Risks.
item.description: President Trump is the first American leader to embrace fighting a full-fledged, joint war with Israel. Washington has tried to avoid that level of coordination in the past.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel at Mar-a-Lago in December.

item.title: Here’s What Happened in the War in the Middle East on Friday
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Friday would be the most intense day of the U.S. air campaign in Iran as a vital oil supply route remained choked off.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A resident walks through a neighborhood of central Beirut, the capital of Lebanon, on Friday.

item.title: U.S. Attacks Iran’s Kharg Island, a Key Port for Oil Exporting, Trump Says
item.description: The strikes on Kharg Island targeted Iranian military forces, not economic infrastructure on the island, a U.S. military official said.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Farnaz Fassihi and Liam Stack
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Planet Labs PBC, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A satellite image of Kharg Island in Iran last month.

item.title: Messi Meets Trump and Argentina Is Divided
item.description: Lionel Messi, the Argentine soccer star, accompanied his team, Inter Miami, to the White House. The event turned into political dynamite back in Argentina.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Daniel Politi
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Lionel Messi and other members of Inter Miami in Washington last week.

item.title: U.S. Vows to Block Iran’s Attempt to Shut Down Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz were “something we are dealing with.” And about 2,500 Marines were headed to the Middle East to bolster the war effort.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Luke Broadwater and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands marched amid airstrikes on Quds Day, a government-sponsored event, on Friday in Tehran.

item.title: A New Trump Envoy Stirs Fears of U.S. Meddling In Brazil’s Elections
item.description: Darren Beattie, the U.S. policy adviser on Brazil, planned to visit former President Jair Bolsonaro in prison and meet his son, who is also seeking the presidency.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times
item.media:description: Darren Beattie, who recently became the State Department’s new senior adviser on Brazil policy, was fired from a job in the first Trump administration for appearing at a conference attended by white nationalists.

item.title: Britain warns its citizens in Dubai they could be jailed for sharing photos of damage from airstrikes.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Dearden
item.media:credit: Altaf Qadri/Associated Press
item.media:description: A building in Dubai damaged by debris from an intercepted missile on Friday. The United Arab Emirates prosecutor said last week that taking or sharing videos documenting airstrikes or damage could be illegal.

item.title: More Marines and Warships Being Sent to Middle East, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: Iran’s response to days of aerial bombardment and long-range artillery strikes has proved more resilient than Trump administration officials anticipated.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Navy ships in Nagasaki, Japan, in 2023.

item.title: U.S.-Made Launcher Fired Missiles From Bahrain Toward Iran, Video Shows
item.description: It is unclear from the video alone whether the U.S. or Bahraini military launched the missiles. Iran has frequently accused Persian Gulf countries of allowing their territory to be used as a launchpad for U.S. attacks.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Aric Toler and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: The Strait of Hormuz is narrow — and shallow enough to lay minefields.
item.dc:creator: Samuel Granados, John Ismay and Agnes Chang

item.title: Read Some of John F. Burns’s Reporting From Around the World
item.description: In a 40-year career as an international correspondent for The New York Times, Mr. Burns had a talent for capturing the sweep of history in intricate detail.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Ozier Muhammad/The New York Times
item.media:description: John F. Burns in 2002 in Afghanistan. Following the attacks of Sept. 11, he coordinated coverage and reported on the war from Kabul and Islamabad, in Pakistan.

item.title: Trump’s Move to Seize Oil Tankers Costs the U.S. Tens of Millions of Dollars
item.description: Although President Trump said seizing tankers would be a financial boon, the cost of maintaining just one aging ship has already reached $47 million.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Nicholas Nehamas
item.media:credit: Vantor, via Reuters
item.media:description: The oil tanker Skipper, seized by the United States, seen off the coast of Texas near Galveston in December.

item.title: Trump Softens Call for Protesters to Take Over Iran
item.description: President Trump said protesters risk getting shot “right through the head,” a change in tone from his earlier comments that Iranians must seize the chance to take over their government.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump now says Iranians could face threats for protesting against the government, which his war has failed to topple so far.

item.title: Thousands Attend Government Rally in Tehran to Denounce Israel and U.S.
item.description: Several senior Iranian officials showed up at the government-sponsored rally, marking Quds Day, an annual anti-Israel event that was shaken by explosions from the U.S.-Israeli aerial assault.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators pumped their fists in defiance despite a blast near the annual Quds Day rally in Tehran on Friday.

item.title: Israel Drops Leaflets on Beirut with Reminders of Gaza War
item.description: Leaflets dropped over the capital referenced Israel’s “success in Gaza” and urged Lebanese citizens to disarm Hezbollah.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rubble in the northern Gaza Strip, in February. Israel heavily bombed Gaza for two years after the Oct. 7, 2023, attack by Hamas.

item.title: How This Oil Supply Shock Compares With the Embargo of 1973
item.description: Governments have stockpiled oil, and cars are more efficient but the supply shock is global, and there’s no sense of when it’ll end.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Marty Lederhandler/Associated Press
item.media:description: The 1973 oil embargo led to panic buying, and long lines at gas stations. While that isn’t likely now in the United States, the price shock is likely to reverberate through the economy.

item.title: Hegseth says an officer has been appointed to investigate deadly strike on Iranian school.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth announced the appointment of a general officer to lead an investigation on a deadly strike that hit an elementary school in Iran.

item.title: U.S. Tech Giants Flocked to the Persian Gulf. Now They Are Targets.
item.description: Amazon, Google and others struck deals in the Persian Gulf to foot the bill for A.I. development. Iran has now threatened attacks against the companies’ infrastructure in the region.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Paul Mozur
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Smoke rose after a reported Iranian drone strike on a fuel facility of Bahrain International Airport on Thursday. Iranian drones have also struck Amazon’s data center in Bahrain and two others in the U.A.E.

item.title: John F. Burns, Prize-winning Foreign Correspondent for The Times, Dies at 81
item.description: In a 40-year career that brought him two Pulitzers, he reported from trouble spots around the world, eloquently conveying the chaos of war.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.media:credit: Michael Kamber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times international correspondent John F. Burns interviewing people in Baghdad on Dec. 13, 2003, the day the Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein was captured.

item.title: Trump Administration Readies Plans to Dismantle NCAR Research Lab
item.description: Proposals include transferring a supercomputer to the University of Wyoming and shifting a space weather lab to a private company.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Caine Delacy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder, Colo.
